title: set -euo pipefail at top level of a SOURCED library leaks into the caller
error: A sourced lib enables `set -e`/`set -u` globally; the caller's shell inherits it and unrelated code starts failing (e.g. unbound vars, conditionals aborting, pattern loops returning wrong results).
cause: `set -euo pipefail` placed at the top level of a file that is meant to be `source`d applies to the SOURCING shell, not just the library. set -e is also unreliable inside functions/subshells. Observed in lib/data-sandbox.sh where it silently broke its own injection detection.
solution: Libraries (sourced files) must NOT enable strict mode at top level. Handle errors explicitly with return codes (`|| return 1`). If the file also runs directly (self-test/CLI), enable `set -euo pipefail` ONLY inside the direct-execution guard.
example_good:
#!/bin/bash
# library: no top-level strict mode
my_func() { ...; return 0; }
if [[ "${1:-}" == "--test" ]]; then
    set -euo pipefail     # strict only when executed directly
    _self_test; exit $?
fi
detection: grep -lE '^set -[eu]|^set -euo' lib/*.sh on files that are sourced elsewhere; guard test: source the lib in a clean shell and assert $- gained no 'e'/'u'.
